excerpt: The Meccans fashioned countless idols—tradition records 360,000 in number; three held special prominence: Lat, Manat, and Uzza; modern Hindu tradition preserves this triad as Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva.
source_work: Taʾwīl al-Daʿāʾim; historical sources on pre-Islamic Arabia

excerpt: After Jesus's era, the world appeared divided into two principles: good and evil. The dualism crystallized, and the concept of two gods emerged: Ahriman, the deity of malevolence, and Yazdan, the God of all goodness.
source_work: historical and comparative religion sources on Zoroastrianism

excerpt: Before the Prophet's advent, the Arabian peninsula had sunk into profound moral darkness. The Kaʿba stood adorned with 360 idols, each representing the god of a different clan. The Kaʿba's walls displayed images of Abraham and Ishmael, Mary and Jesus.
source_work: early Islamic historical narratives; Sīrat sources

excerpt: The Originator, Most High, belongs to a category that the five senses cannot perceive, and since no attribute adheres to His essence, it is impossible to liken Him to created things. His existence can only be established through logical arguments and demonstrative proofs.
Our Master ʿAlī declared: 'The incapacity to perceive Him is itself a form of perception. And to inquire after the secret of the Essence-that-is-Secret is to commit association with Him (shirk).'
source_work: Nahj al-Balāgha (sayings of ʿAlī, compiled by al-Sharīf al-Raḍī)
source_author: ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib; al-Sharīf al-Raḍī (compiler)
source_location_hint: theological epistemology, sermon on divine transcendence

excerpt: Our Master al-Muʾayyad (Hibat Allāh ibn Mūsā al-Shīrāzī) and Our Master al-Ḥamīd al-Dīn al-Kirmānī stated: 'We stand ensnared in bondage and confined by the straits of our contingency. When we attempt to recall You without that which You transcend, we point to You perforce—a sin, yet connected to forgiveness.'
source_location_hint: mystical theology, doctrine of human limitation

excerpt: There is a distinction between the knower (ʿālim) among knowers and the Absolute Knower (ʿālim muṭlaq). To understand the Creator alone as a knower constitutes association (shirk), for if He were merely a knower, we too are knowers—though our knowledge is inferior. The Absolute Knower is one whose knowledge depends upon no cause or effect.
source_work: al-Masābīḥ fī Ithbāt al-Imāma; Raḥat al-ʿAql

excerpt: Logical analysis proves that Allāh is One, perfect in power, and has no partner. If both were perfect and equal, the existence of one would be redundant. If one were defective, both would be proved defective. Therefore no partnership is possible.

Everything that possesses a body is encompassed within six dimensions and confined to its place. Every body is dependent upon its Creator for continued existence—a dependency opposed to complete self-sufficiency (Samadiyyah) that characterizes the Originator.
Imām Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq declared in assembly: 'Allāh is greater than to be confined to any limit or measure.' When asked, he clarified that this means He transcends all limitation and enumeration.

He created all beings from absolute nothingness, without matter, without substance, and without any medium. Had He created from pre-existent matter or in a place requiring sustenance, His action would be defective—yet Allāh cannot be defective.

Letters are themselves mute; we are their authors, for no letter sounds unless its speaker is present. Thus we attempt to interpret our Creator through His creation—letters—an undertaking doomed to failure.
source_location_hint: negative theology, linguistic critique of divine description

excerpt: Allāh is not a name but the Name. This Name is not the denomination of the Essence, but the name of the First Intellect (ʿAql al-Awwal). The First Intellect possesses ninety-nine attributes, designated by names. These ninety-nine attributes together with the Essence constitute one hundred.
source_location_hint: Fatimid metaphysics, doctrine of divine names

excerpt: Allāh has no opposite. Satan himself believed in Allāh but denied Adam and sought respite. His knowledge availed him naught, for he disobeyed. Allāh proclaims: 'There is no compulsion in religion.' Both paths lie open before us.
source_work: Qurʾān exegesis; tafsīr traditions; theological disputations

excerpt: Allāh possesses all power but does not commit injustice. He refrains not because He is unable to wrong, but because wrongdoing contradicts His glory and majesty.
source_author: Islamic theologians; Muʿtazilite and Ashʿarite schools

excerpt: Sincerity is that through which deliverance comes. Sincerity is the fruit of unwavering certainty (yaqīn). Sincerity is the fruit of worship. Sincerity is the noblest of deeds; no deed can be noblest save through knowledge. The most exalted knowledge is the knowledge of monotheism.
source_author: ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib (al-Sharīf al-Raḍī, compiler)

excerpt: The foundation of religion is knowledge of Him—acknowledgment that He is the Creator. The reality of such knowledge is His tawhīd. The system of tawhīd is for the sound intellect to bear witness that He is free from all attributes, for every attribute and the one described by it testify to their created nature.

There is no faith without sincerity, no sincerity without knowledge, no knowledge without affirmation, no affirmation without tawhīd, no tawhīd without sincerity, and sincerity cannot coexist with anthropomorphism (tashbīh), for anthropomorphism entails attributing characteristics to Him.

The Originator is exalted and sanctified beyond all such restrictions and limitations. What enters imagination becomes likened to something, and what is encompassed by imagination loses transcendence. The Originator transcends all such constraints.

He is the Agent of every action, yet His action occurs without direct physical contact (mubāsharat). His guidance comes without sign. His word is without articulation. His Face is wherever you turn. His path is the Uprightness of Religion. He explains us to ourselves and teaches us through ourselves.
source_author: ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib; Nāṣir-i Khusraw

excerpt: Someone asked him to make us know Allah. He replied: 'He who knows his own soul has done it by his Lord.'
source_location_hint: On the hadith 'man ʿarafa nafsahu faqad ʿarafa rabbahu'

excerpt: When a person reflects upon how the Almighty created him from nutritive elements and extract of clay, then transforming it through stages, he recognizes that none of these gifts arose from his own effort.
source_location_hint: On human creation and recognition of the Creator
